title: Anger at the Friend Whose Mother Is Still Alive
dek: This article explores the intense, often unspoken anger experienced when a friend complains about her still-living mother. It delves into the physical and emotional responses that arise, highlighting societal taboos around expressing such feelings, and draws on Thich Nhat Hanh's teachings about the body as a source of true understanding.
bodyMarkdown:
## The visceral reaction when she complains about her mother


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.092Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You’re sitting across from her in the dim corner of the café, the late afternoon sun filtering in through the dusty windowpanes, dust motes floating like slow-fireflies in the golden light. She launches into another litany about her mother - how controlling, how cold, how impossible to please. The words unfurl, and with them, it happens: that sudden, fierce heat blooms across your face, the rush of blood like a live coal pressed against your cheekbones. Your masseter tightens, the jaw clamp locking down before you can stop it, the teeth grinding into an unspoken clenched vow: don’t lose it here. But the body remembers. The jaw remembers the old rules, the old silences.

You try to breathe, but the diaphragm catches, hitching just beneath your ribs, the breath shallow and sharp, like the snap of a twig underfoot in a quiet forest. Your trapezius muscles rise, taut ribbons from neck to shoulder, a silent armor you didn’t ask for. In this moment, your body is shouting - loud and unrelenting - while your mind scrambles to find the polite nod or the neutral “I see.” But you don’t see. You feel the heat, the pressure, the raw edge of fury that tastes like betrayal, like grief.

Her mother is still alive. This is the rub. She is still here, occupying space, breathing oxygen, still able to speak and to wound. And you? You were taught to swallow this anger, to hide it behind a smile or a soft word, especially when it’s directed at the living, at the mother. Society stamped it into your fascia: anger at a mother is ugly, ungrateful, taboo. But here you are, the body revolt making itself known in the tightening of your levator scapulae, the subtle but insistent clench that says no, no, no.

Thich Nhat Hanh taught that the body is the ground of true understanding, that the body knows what the mind is still fumbling to grasp. So listen - listen to the way your scalp prickles, the way the back of your neck burns with a quiet fury. This is not pathology. This is biology. This is the body’s insistence that what you are feeling matters. The anger is not a moral failure; it is a messenger, a signal flaring up in the very muscles that carry your history. It is the unspoken story of what you’ve endured, the years of silent suffering, the invisible scars etched in muscle and fascia.

You smell the faint musk of your own skin under stress, a salty, iron scent that rises as the seconds pass. The room seems to shrink, the air thickening with unspoken words and the weight of inherited pain. Your body tightens in protest while your mind juggles the polite response: sympathy, understanding, “I'm sorry.” But the body knows the truth that your words cannot yet touch. You are angry. At her. At the mother who is still alive. At the cruel, tangled knot of family wounds that refuse to unravel quietly.

This is your body speaking. This is your anger claiming its space, demanding acknowledgment in the very muscles that carry your history. The heat in your face, the clench in your jaw, the rising tension in your neck - they are not enemies to be silenced. They are the raw, unvarnished language of your lived experience, insisting on being heard.

## The Anatomy of Grief-Anger: Where It Lives in the Body

You feel it first in the psoas. That deep, unyielding coil of muscle, nestled behind the abdomen, anchoring your spine to your legs. It contracts without your permission, tightening like a clenched fist in the depths of your body. This is not just tension  -  it is a visceral refusal, a bodily scream lodged beneath your ribs. The psoas remembers every moment you’ve swallowed your fury, every time you envied the friend whose mother still answers her calls, whose mother’s voice hasn’t slipped into the silence of goodbye.

Your diaphragm, the dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs that usually rises and falls with effortless grace, freezes. It clamps down, a gate slammed shut against the rawness of your grief and rage. You try to breathe, but the breath is shallow, thin, scraping along the edges of your chest like dry autumn leaves skittering across cracked pavement. This is the body in mourning, the body caught between loss and longing, anger and helplessness. The diaphragm’s freeze is not a failure of will; it’s your biology saying, “This is too much right now.”

You might notice your trapezius, the thick, triangular muscle spanning from neck to shoulder, knotting into a solid block of tension. The levator scapulae, those small muscles that lift your shoulder blades, stiffen, pulling your posture into a defensive hunch. Your jaw clenches  -  the masseter muscle tightens so firmly that your teeth grind in the quiet hours of the night, when the world has gone soft and you are left with the hard edges of your fury. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is doing what it knows how to do: protect, resist, survive.

The smell of your skin changes subtly at these moments. There’s a coppery tang, the faint metallic scent of adrenaline mixing with the natural musk of your sweat. It’s the smell of a body on alert, wired to respond to a threat that isn’t a stranger in the dark but a quiet, aching injustice: the friend whose mother’s voice still carries warmth on the other end of the line, whose mother’s absence you cannot claim, whose loss you never get to mourn in the same way. Your breath catches again, trapped beneath the diaphragm’s frozen dome, a reminder that grief’s anger is not polite, not neat. It is raw muscle and shallow breath, the body’s unsent letter to the world.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma, anger, grief - they don’t just live in the mind. They settle in the flesh, the fascia, the muscles that shape how we carry ourselves through the day. Your psoas’s coil, your diaphragm’s freeze, your jaw’s clenched protest - they are the anatomy of your grief-anger. Not a failure, not a flaw, but a message from the body that something vital is being held, something that demands your attention.

## The Illusion of Fairness: Why the Nervous System Perceives Her Mother’s Existence as a Threat

You sit across from her, the friend whose mother still breathes and is present in her life, and an unfamiliar tightness coils low in your belly, the psoas muscle spasming in a subtle but insistent protest. You want to feel relief, or at least neutrality, but instead, your nervous system flares with a quiet, ingrained alarm. It’s not about fairness in the conscious mind; fairness is a human concept, a mental construct. What’s happening is deeper, older, stitched into your biology before you ever learned the word “fair.” Your body is reacting to a threat it cannot name but knows intimately: attachment, survival, and the haunting absence of the very thing your friend still has.

The survival circuitry in your brain - particularly the amygdala and the brainstem - registers attachment loss as a primal threat. Attachment, as studied by researchers like Allan Schore and elaborated upon by Bessel van der Kolk, is not just emotional; it is the foundation of your nervous system’s regulation. The presence of a caregiver’s reliable touch, voice, and scent during infancy calibrates your ability to feel safe in the world. When that presence is cut off, your brain’s alarm bells ring with the urgency of a fire drill. You learned early that her mother’s existence, her ongoing presence, signals a reality you no longer inhabit. Your nervous system perceives this as a threat because it is a constant reminder of what you lost - or never had.

This is not about envy or petty comparison. It’s about the biology of attachment trauma, which Robert Augustus Masters describes as the “unhealed wound of connection.” Your body remembers the unmet need for soothing, for the soft hum of someone’s breath against your skin late at night, the subtle release of oxytocin when a mother’s hand strokes the trapezius muscle along your neck, easing the tension you didn’t know was there. The friend’s mother is alive, and this fact triggers your dorsal vagal freeze - the ancient freeze reflex that immobilizes when escape is impossible. You feel frozen in the presence of this living proof of what you lack.

Thich Nhat Hanh’s teachings remind us that the body holds the story before the words reach the mind. The illusory fairness your mind seeks - the idea that everyone should have the same love, the same presence - collides with the stubborn fact that biology does not negotiate. Your nervous system is wired to protect you, to keep you alive, and in its calculus, her mother’s survival is a constant threat to your fragile equilibrium. This is why your jaw tightens - the masseter clenched as if to hold back a scream. This is why your shoulders rise involuntarily, the levator scapulae twitching in a silent protest. It’s not just emotional; it’s fascial, muscular, a body-wide message that your safety has been compromised.

You smell the faint, almost imperceptible trace of chamomile from the tea you brewed earlier, a bitter-sweet reminder of the herbs that can soothe your autonomic storm but cannot erase the lived reality of your body’s grief. Karla McLaren’s work on emotional intelligence teaches us that anger here is not a moral failing; it’s a messenger from your nervous system, signaling that your attachment needs were never met. The friend’s mother is alive, and your nervous system’s threat response is a testament to the enduring impact of early attachment wounds - not an indictment of your character or your friendship.

This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body remembers what your mind tries to deny: presence and absence are not equal, and the living proof of that imbalance is a shock to the system. It is the body’s way of saying, “I am still here, still wounded, still needing.” Your anger toward that friend is the shadow cast by the biology of attachment - the silent, aching echo of your own nervous system’s unquiet history.

## Gabor Maté on Attachment and Authenticity in the Face of Loss

You carry it in the nape of your neck, that tight coil that winds through the levator scapulae muscles just behind your ears, the place where your body first learned that some losses are too big to name. When you look at your friend whose mother is still alive - the one whose laughter fills the room on weekends, whose voice still carries the weight of shared history - you feel it like a slow-burning ache in your diaphragm. This isn’t just envy or spite; it’s the body’s ledger, the score of what you never got. Gabor Maté teaches us that attachment is the architecture of our emotional selves, the invisible threads that tether us to what once was, or what should have been, and how those threads can twist into knots of shame and rage when loss is complicated, incomplete, or denied.

Maté’s work reminds us that anger isn’t a failure of character but a signal that authenticity is under siege. The body remembers the absent mother’s arms as an ache in the psoas, that deep core muscle that sighs silently under stress. It holds the tension of unmet needs in the trapezius, a constant, low-grade fire burning between your shoulder blades. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is a witness to the losses you never spoke aloud - the birthdays missed, the consolations withheld, the silent rooms where grief went underground.

You learned early that expressing this anger was dangerous. Maybe your fury was met with silence, or worse, dismissal. The muscles learned to clench and hold, the fascia tightened like a drumskin over your ribcage. Maté’s reminder that authenticity requires safety is crucial here: without a safe container, your body armor thickens. You breathe shallow and fast, the diaphragm tethered tight, and your voice feels caught in the masseter muscles, clenched and unspoken.

But there is also a path through this embodied grief. Maté’s teachings invite you to witness your inner landscape with a fierce tenderness - acknowledging the raw edges of your anger without shame. You might sense it as a slow unwinding in the occipital ridge at the base of your skull, a loosening of the tension that has kept your jaw clenched for decades. This isn’t about forcing forgiveness or pretending the loss was fair. It’s about reclaiming the authenticity that your body has guarded all along.

Herbs like skullcap or passionflower can gently ease the nervous system into a state where this witnessing feels possible, where the body’s memory can shift from defensive contraction to curious openness. Maté’s integration of neuroscience, attachment theory, and compassionate presence offers a map for navigating the terrain of anger that arises when your friend’s mother still laughs while yours is gone silent. Your body knows this terrain intimately, and in that knowing, there is the possibility of profound healing.

## The Herbal Support: Nervines and Adaptogens for the Grieving, Angry Body

You sit in the damp quiet of early morning, the sky just a bruise of dark blue, and your body hums with a tension that’s neither fully rage nor pure grief. It’s the ache of watching someone else’s loss feel like a betrayal, the rawness of anger that tastes like iron on your tongue and sets your diaphragm tight like a noose. This is where herbs come in - not as some airy remedy but as flesh and blood medicine, reaching into the muscles and fascia where your fury festers.

Motherwort, Leonurus cardiaca, is the first ally to call in. It’s named for the heart, but not the sentimental kind - the actual cardiac muscle, the organ that tightens and spasms under emotional assault. Motherwort works on the vagus nerve, that wandering messenger between your brain and body, smoothing the spasms of the heart and calming the hyperactive sympathetic nervous system that’s been driving your trapezius and levator scapulae into knots. If your jaw clenches so hard the masseter muscle burns or your neck feels like it’s been wrapped in barbed wire, motherwort’s bitters and alkaloids ease those contractions. Its subtle bitterness nudges the liver’s detox pathways, helping you process the toxins of rage that your body refuses to release. It is not a sedative in the dulling sense but a tender guide, reminding your body that it can soften even while holding fire.

Skullcap, Scutellaria lateriflora, enters the scene with a different kind of medicine - cool, clear, and steady. It’s the herb of the nervous system’s fascia, the connective tissue that envelopes every nerve ending and muscle fiber, including the psoas, your fight-or-flight muscle buried deep in the abdomen. When you’re angry at a friend whose mother still breathes, your psoas might clench like a fist around your guts, holding in all the unspoken accusations and grief. Skullcap’s flavonoids work on the GABA receptors, the brain’s natural brake system, but more than that, they lubricate the fascia and calm the peripheral nerves. You can almost smell the faint, earthy sweetness of skullcap as it settles into the nervous tissue, easing the prickling tension that sits just beneath your skin. It doesn’t erase the anger; it simply gives your body the capacity to hold it without shattering.

Hawthorn, Crataegus monogyna, is the final piece of this triad. It is the medicine of the broken heart, the physical heart that aches when the mind cannot make sense of loss. Hawthorn’s berries, leaves, and flowers are rich in proanthocyanidins, compounds that strengthen the blood vessels and improve circulation. When your chest feels tight, when the diaphragm refuses to drop and the breath is shallow, hawthorn gently coaxes the heart to open. It is the herb for the grief that has hardened into anger, the anger that is just grief with its teeth bared. You might take it as a strong infusion, tasting the tart, slightly astringent notes that remind you of the complexity of your own pain. Hawthorn says: your heart is strong enough to hold this.

These herbs are not a cure for the fact that her mother is alive and yours is not. They are not a spiritual bypass. They are biological support for a body that is doing its best to survive an impossible emotional landscape. They work on the tissues, the nerves, the blood, reminding your body that it is safe to feel the fury, safe to feel the grief, and safe, eventually, to let the breath drop back down into the belly.

## Karla McLaren on Anger as the Boundary-Restoring Emotion

You feel it in the sudden heat that flushes your cheeks, the way your masseter muscle locks your jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. Your friend is talking about her mother again - the mother who is still here, still breathing, still capable of calling on a Sunday afternoon. And there it is: the anger. It rises not as a gentle wave but as a sharp, metallic tang in the back of your throat, a sudden rigidity in your levator scapulae that pulls your shoulders up to your ears. You were taught to call this jealousy, to shame yourself for it, to swallow it down until it sits like a stone in your gut. But Karla McLaren’s work on emotional intelligence offers a different map. She teaches that anger is not a monster to be slain; it is the boundary-restoring emotion, the honorable sentry of your psyche.

McLaren reminds us that anger arises when a boundary has been crossed, when something vital has been threatened or lost. In this moment, the boundary isn’t physical; it’s the fragile, invisible line around your grief. Your friend’s casual complaints about her living mother trespass on the sacred, silent space where your own mother’s absence lives. Your body’s reaction - the clenched jaw, the frozen diaphragm, the psoas tightening like a coiled spring - is not a sign that you are a bad friend. It is your biology’s way of saying, “This hurts. This is too close to the wound.” Your anger is actually trying to protect your grief.

Think of the way your body smells when this anger hits - a sudden, sharp scent of adrenaline, the musk of a nervous system preparing to fight. This is the body’s ancient wisdom at work. McLaren suggests that instead of suppressing this energy, we must ask it what it needs to protect. Your anger is standing guard over the raw, tender ache of your loss. It is demanding that the reality of your mother’s absence be respected, even if only by you. When you try to smile and nod, when you force your trapezius to relax while your heart is racing, you are abandoning the very part of yourself that needs protection.

This is not pathology; this is biology. The anger is a messenger, insisting that your grief is valid, that the disparity between your friend’s reality and yours is a real and painful truth. McLaren’s approach invites you to welcome this anger, to feel its heat without letting it burn down the room. You don’t have to lash out at your friend, but you do have to acknowledge the sentry. You have to feel the tension in your jaw and say, “I hear you. I know this isn’t fair.”

By honoring the anger as a protector, you allow the body to complete its cycle. The masseter can begin to soften, the diaphragm can finally drop, and the breath can return to the belly. The anger doesn’t disappear, but it transforms from a rigid, shameful secret into a fierce, compassionate guardian of your grief. It is the body’s way of ensuring that your loss is never forgotten, never minimized, and always held with the fierce respect it deserves.

## The Somatic Practice: Moving the Stagnant Grief-Anger

You feel it sitting heavy in your chest, a dense, unyielding knot right where the diaphragm meets the ribs. It’s the stagnant grief-anger, the fury that has nowhere to go when your friend talks about her living mother. It’s the tension that pulls your levator scapulae tight, hiking your shoulders up as if to shield your ears from words you cannot bear to hear. This practice is not about making the anger disappear; it’s about giving it a channel, a way to move through the fascia and muscle so it doesn’t calcify in your bones.

Begin by standing. Feel the solid, unforgiving floor beneath your feet. Press your heels down, then the balls of your feet, then your toes. Imagine the bones of your feet widening, claiming space. This is grounding. Your nervous system needs to know that the ground will hold you, even when the unfairness of the world feels like it might swallow you whole. Notice the psoas, that deep core muscle that has been gripping your spine in a silent panic. As you press your feet into the floor, invite the psoas to soften, just a fraction. You don’t have to force it; just offer the suggestion of release.

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. The masseter muscle is likely clenched, holding back the words you were taught never to say. Place your fingertips gently on the hinge of your jaw, right in front of your ears. Press lightly, feeling the hard ridge of muscle. Take a breath in, letting the air fill the tight space beneath your ribs. As you exhale, let your jaw drop open. Make a sound. It doesn’t have to be loud; it can be a low, guttural sigh, a hum, or a growl. Let the sound vibrate through the bones of your face. This is the vocalization of the unfairness. It is the body’s way of saying, “This is not right, and I am angry.”

Do this again. Inhale, feeling the ribs expand, stretching the intercostal muscles that have been locked tight. Exhale, drop the jaw, and let the sound out. Notice the smell of your own breath, the metallic tang of adrenaline beginning to dissipate. Feel the heat in your face, the flush of blood moving, no longer trapped in a silent flush of shame. You are letting the body speak the truth that the mind has been trying to polite away.

Finally, bring your hands to your chest, right over the sternum. Press firmly. Feel the steady, stubborn beat of your own heart. This is the heart that survives the unfairness, the heart that holds both the grief of your mother’s absence and the fierce, protective anger that guards it. Breathe into your hands. Feel the slackening of the masseter, the softening of the belly, the warmth returning to your feet. This is the beginning of moving stagnant grief-anger, a quiet rebellion against the old rules that told you to hide this fury away. Your body knows what to do. You just need to listen.

## FAQ: Questions and Answers about Anger at the Friend Whose Mother Is Still Alive

**Q: Why do I feel this sharp, raw anger at my friend just because their mother is still alive? Isn’t that selfish or petty?**  
A: This anger is not about your friend or their mother in any simple way. It’s a raw, unfiltered pulse of loss and longing lodged deep in your diaphragm and psoas, a visceral ache that your body interprets as rage. Your jaw clenches - masseter tightening like a steel trap - because your nervous system can’t find words for the grief that’s too tangled to speak. This is not selfish; it’s biology. The body remembers what the mind can’t hold. When you watch your friend laugh on the phone with her mother, or see their effortless calls home, your nervous system flares with the memory of your own empty line. You’re angry because your body smells the absence, the invisible cord snapped. You are not a bad person for this. You are human.

**Q: How do I stop feeling guilty for being angry at my friend? I feel like I’m betraying them.**  
A: Guilt is the brain’s way of shushing your body’s raw message. It’s a social anesthetic telling you to hide the parts of your pain that feel “unacceptable.” But your anger isn’t a betrayal; it’s a shadow cast by the light of your own loss. Think of your trapezius muscle, how it tightens around your neck in the late-night hours, twisting your breath off in the cold, damp air. That tension is the body’s way of saying, “I’m hurting here.” You can carry love for your friend and still carry this anger without guilt. The two don’t cancel each other out. As Karla McLaren teaches, anger is a messenger, not a monster. It’s telling you something you need to hear about your own unmet needs and grief.

**Q: Will my friend ever understand this anger? Should I try to explain it to them?**  
A: Often, your friend’s body is holding a different story, one that can’t fully hold or reflect your pain. Their mother’s continued presence in their life is a living, breathing difference between your experiences. This isn’t a failing on their part, nor a judgment on yours. Explaining your anger might feel like trying to catch smoke with your bare hands, but it can open a space for honest connection if you want it. Choose a moment when your diaphragm isn’t clenched so tightly it cuts off your voice, when you can speak softly but firmly about how the absence of your own mother is still raw inside your ribs. Pema Chödrön reminds us that vulnerability isn’t weakness; it’s the courage to show the parts of ourselves that ache. Your friend may not fully grasp the depth of your loss, but witnessing your truth can deepen your bond.

**Q: How do I hold this anger in my body without letting it consume me or ruin my friendship?**  
A: Holding anger means learning to be with it, not pushing it into your levator scapulae or collapsing it into your diaphragm like a stone in your chest. Resmaa Menakem’s work with racialized trauma shows us the power of somatic awareness - feeling the exact shape, temperature, and weight of your anger in your body without judgment. You might find gentle herbal allies like chamomile or passionflower to soften your nervous system’s overdrive, but the real work is in the slow, patient tracking of sensations: the twitch in your jaw at midnight, the quickened pulse in your wrists when your friend mentions their mother. Naming these sensations aloud or in a journal makes the invisible visible. This isn’t a quick fix - this is a practice of presence, a way to honor your fury as a living part of your healing.

**Q: Can I ever move beyond this anger, or is it something I must carry forever?**  
A: The body doesn’t erase loss; it carries it, layers of fascia and muscle memory stitching your story into your flesh. But anger’s shape can change. It can shift from a tight, unbearable knot in your masseter and trapezius to a slow-burning fire that fuels your resilience. Gabor Maté reminds us that the body knows how to heal when it’s allowed to express without shame. Over time, with patience and kindness toward your body’s signals, this anger can become a source of strength rather than isolation. It’s not about forgetting or moving on - it’s about learning to live with your story held tenderly in your cells, alongside your friendship, without the burn of guilt or silence.

---

Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.

In our work on anger at the friend whose mother is still alive, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
